coredata.dc:title: Oxygen-rich TiO2 decorated with C-Dots: Highly efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts in degradations of different contaminants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Feizpoor, Solmaz
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Ahadzadeh, Iraj
Yubuta, Kunio
coredata.dc:description: Providing novel photocatalysts with high photocatalytic efficiency is of great significance. In the present work, hydrogen peroxide and carbon dots (C-Dots) were utilized to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 under visible light. The fabricated TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts were analyzed by XRD, HRTEM, SEM, EDX, BET, FT-IR, XPS, PL, UV–Vis DRS, EIS, and photocurrent density. Photocatalytic abilities of the nanocomposites were evaluated by photocatalytic removal of RhB, MO, MB, fuchsine, and Cr (VI) upon visible-light illumination. The results demonstrated that the binary nanocomposites exhibited remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity compared with the TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo photocatalysts. The best photocatalytic performance was obtained using 0.75 mL of C-Dots, which was approximately 79.2, 17.1, 71.4, and 40.5 times higher than the pure TiO2 for degradations of RhB, MO, MB, and fuchsine, respectively. Furthermore, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites exhibited high stability in consecutive photocatalytic processes. Based on the results, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalyst is expected to become a promising photocatalyst for practical applications in water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots
Visible-light-driven photocatalyst
TiO2
Water pollutants

item-info.ce:copyright: The Society of Powder Technology Japan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites with different compositions.
(a) EDX spectra for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. (b-e) EDX mapping of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM and (b) HRTEM images of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra for the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite: (a) Survey scan and high-resolution spectra for: (b) Ti 2p, (c) O 1s, and (d) C 1s.

(a) UV–vis DR spectra for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, C-Dots, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots samples with different compositions. (b) FT-IR spectra for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, C-Dots, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples.

(a) Photodegradation of RhB over the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, TiO2/C-Dots (0.75 mL), and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites with different compositions. UV–vis spectra for degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation over the (b) TiO2, (c) TiO2-peroxo, and (d) TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, TiO2/C-Dots (0.75 mL), and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites. (b) PL spectra for the TiO2, TiO2-peoxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite. (c) Transient photocurrent for TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. (d) EIS Nyquist plots of TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. (e) Nitrogen sorption data for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. (f) Pore size distribution curves for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples.
The upconverted PL spectra of the as-synthesized CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Plots of (αhν)2 versus hν for the TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo samples. Mott–Schottky plots for the (b) TiO2 and (c) TiO2-peroxo samples.
A plausible mechanism for separation of electron-hole pairs in the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites.
The degradation rate constants of RhB over the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite in the presence of various scavengers.
Reusability of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite.
The degradation rate constants of RhB, fuchsine, MO, and MB, and photoreduce reaction of Cr(VI) over the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples under visible-light irradiation.
Preparation procedure for the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites.
Experimental weight percentages for the elements in the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposites.
Textural properties of the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Oxygen-rich TiO2 decorated with C-Dots: Highly efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts in degradations of different contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University, 2-1-1 Katahira, Aoba-ku, Sendai 980-8577, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Providing novel photocatalysts with high photocatalytic efficiency is of great significance. In the present work, hydrogen peroxide and carbon dots (C-Dots) were utilized to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 under visible light. The fabricated TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts were analyzed by XRD, HRTEM, SEM, EDX, BET, FT-IR, XPS, PL, UV–Vis DRS, EIS, and photocurrent density. Photocatalytic abilities of the nanocomposites were evaluated by photocatalytic removal of RhB, MO, MB, fuchsine, and Cr (VI) upon visible-light illumination. The results demonstrated that the binary nanocomposites exhibited remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity compared with the TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo photocatalysts. The best photocatalytic performance was obtained using 0.75 mL of C-Dots, which was approximately 79.2, 17.1, 71.4, and 40.5 times higher than the pure TiO2 for degradations of RhB, MO, MB, and fuchsine, respectively. Furthermore, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites exhibited high stability in consecutive photocatalytic processes. Based on the results, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalyst is expected to become a promising photocatalyst for practical applications in water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural waters are primarily polluted by organic and inorganic contaminants, which have caused great damage to the environment and human health [1–4]. Therefore, it is important to explore an effective and advanced method to minimize or neutralize the environmental harm of these pollutants. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis has been considered as one of the efficient methods to tackle environmental pollution and energy crises [5–10]. As compared to other semiconductors, titanium dioxide (TiO2) plays a considerable role in the field of environmental protection applications, owing to its high oxidation ability, low cost, environmental-friendly nature, and photochemical stability [11–18]. However, the photonic efficiency of TiO2 is poor under visible light, because it can only absorb UV light. To solve this drawback, various approaches have been proposed, like doping metals or non-metals to the structure of TiO2, dye photosensitization, and combining TiO2 with other semiconducting materials to form heterojunctions [19–29].

Besides the above approaches, interaction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with TiO2 to synthesize TiO2-peroxo is an effective approach to boost its photocatalytic performance under visible light. By H2O2 treatment, band gap energy of TiO2 is remarkably decreased, resulting in considerable absorption in visible region [30–33]. Recently, carbon dots (C-Dots) as a carbon nanomaterial have attracted significant research attention in the photodegradation field, due to their excellent electronic, optical, and upconversion photoluminescence properties. C-Dots can suggest promising benefits in the photocatalytic systems by converting lower-energy lights to higher-energy lights, enabling more absorption of the visible spectrum of solar light [34–37].

Based on this background, in this work, TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts were prepared by a simple procedure. Then, the fabricated photocatalysts were fully characterized and they revealed exceptional photocatalytic ability for removal of rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO), methylene (MB), fuchsine, and photoreduction of Cr (VI). The free radical quenching tests displayed that
OH and
O2 − radicals have crucial role in the RhB degradation. The photoluminescence (PL) and photocurrent analyses were carried out to confirm the effective separation of charge carriers in the binary TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts. The reusability tests of the photocatalyst have also been performed. Eventually, a probable mechanism for the improved photocatalytic performance of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts was proposed.
The detailed description for characterization methods included in the supporting information.

For synthesis of the TiO2-peroxo photocatalyst, 4 mL titanium (IV) butoxide (Loba Chemie, 99%) was added into 30 mL H2O2 (Loba Chemie, wt. 30%) solution (1 M) followed by 60 min of stirring. The obtained product was collected, washed by water and ethyl alcohol and dried. Then, the formed yellow solid was calcined at 400 °C for 120 min [31].

The C-Dots was synthesized according to a procedure described previously [38]. For preparation of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample, 0.4 g the TiO2-peroxo sample and 0.75 mL of the C-Dots aqueous solution (5 mg/mL) were added in 150 mL water and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then, the resultant suspension was dried. The TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts with 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mL of the C-Dots solutions were synthesized by similar procedure (Scheme 1 ).

The photocatalytic performances were investigated by the removal of MB, MO, RhB with concentration of 1.0 × 10−5 M and fuchsine (0.77 × 10−5 M) and photoreduction of Cr(VI) (100 mg/L). The visible light was performed by a 50 W LED lamp. Emission spectrum of this source has been reported elsewhere [39]. Before illumination, the reaction mixture containing 0.1 g of the photocatalyst and 250 mL of pollutants were kept at dark for 1 h to achieve equilibrium. At predefined times, the suspensions were collected and concentrations of MB, MO, RhB, fuchsine, and Cr (VI) were determined at wavelengths of 664, 477, 553, 540, and 540 nm, respectively. The recycling study was performed in order to evaluate the photo-stability of the photocatalyst and examined for 4 runs. The 1st cycle of the experiment was carried out as indicated in the above procedure under visible-light irradiation. After completion of the 1st run, the suspension was centrifuged to obtain the photocatalyst, which was washed two times with deionized water and ethyl alcohol, and dried at 60 °C for 24 h and then applied for the next run. To perform the 2nd run, the used photocatalyst with the fresh RhB solution was mixed. The same study was repeated for 3rd, and 4th runs.

Crystalline structures of the as-prepared photocatalysts were explored by XRD measurements, as shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction angles of TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo samples can be related to the tetragonal structure of TiO2 (JCPDS file no. 04-0477) [31]. For the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites, due to the low content and high dispersion of C-Dots over TiO2, no distinct characteristic peaks of C-Dots were observed [40].

Morphology and microstructure of the as-synthesized TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst were studied by SEM and HRTEM images. As observed in SEM image (Fig. 3 a), the photocatalyst has spherical-like particles with high aggregation. Fig. 3b exhibits HRTEM image of the nanocomposite. The lattice spacing of 0.352 and 0.320 nm attributed to the (1 0 1) crystallographic plane of tetragonal phase TiO2 and (0 0 2) plane of C-Dots [27,37], confirming integration of C-Dots with TiO2-peroxo.

The composition and chemical oxidation states of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst were detected by XPS analysis and the spectra are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, the Ti 2p, O 1s, and C 1s signals are seen from the full survey spectrum of the nanocomposite. The two symmetric peaks located at 458.9 and 464.5 eV in Fig. 4b are related to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, confirming the presence of Ti4+ ions [41]. Furthermore, O 1s peaks are located at 530.6 and 532.8 eV (Fig. 4c), which can be ascribed to the oxygen of TiO and H2O [41]. The spectrum of C 1S (Fig. 4d) displays two peaks at 285.0 and 288.8 eV, which can be ascribed to CC and CO bonds, respectively [37,42]. Therefore, the XPS analysis confirmed that TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite was successfully synthesized.

To investigate the optical behavior of the photocatalysts, the UV–vis DR spectra were provided. As represented in Fig. 5 a, the pristine TiO2 possesses absorption edge in the UV region. After H2O2 treatment, peroxo is introduced into the surface of TiO2, resulting in boosted absorption in visible region for the TiO2-peroxo sample [31]. Furthermore, integration of C-Dots on the surface of the TiO2-peroxo photocatalyst caused considerable optical response in the visible range. It can be deduced that visible-light harvesting ability may be one of the parameters related to the boosted photocatalytic performance of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots samples. The FT-IR spectra of the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, C-Dots, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots samples are displayed in Fig. 5b. The observed bands at 400–600 and 3400–3600 cm−1 regions are attributed to the TiO and OH bonds, respectively [43]. For the TiO2-peroxo and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts, the band at 900 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of OO bond [30]. The peaks at 1400 and 1600 cm−1 in the C-Dots and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposite are assigned to the CC and CO stretching vibration modes from C-Dots counterparts, respectively [38].

For investigating the photocatalytic performances of the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts, RhB degradation experiments were performed under visible light. As Fig. 6 a displays, without adding any photocatalyst, no significant photodegradation of RhB was observed upon visible-light illumination. As a comparison, the pure TiO2 and TiO2/C-Dots (0.75 mL) were also utilized as the photocatalysts. It is obvious that the photocatalytic activity of the pristine TiO2 and TiO2/C-Dots (0.75 mL) were not satisfactory. While the TiO2-peroxo showed very higher photodegradation performance than the pure TiO2. Interestingly, when C-Dots were integrated with the TiO2-peroxo powder, the photocatalytic performances of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposites were further enhanced. By increasing C-Dots content from 0.25 mL to 0.75 mL, the removal efficiency of RhB by the binary photocatalysts was substantially enhanced, but declined when the C-Dots content increased from 0.75 mL to 1 mL. The results obviously illustrated that the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite exhibited the best photocatalytic performance. The decrease of the photocatalytic performance with adding more CDs is related to aggregation of its particles over the TiO2-peroxo photocatalyst, which leads to hindering efficiently separation of the charge carriers. Moreover, the arrangement of the photocatalytic performances are as TiO2 < TiO2/C-Dots (0.75 mL) < TiO2-peroxo < TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.25 mL) < TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (1 mL) < TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.5 mL) < TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL). Fig. 6(b-d) demonstrated the variations in RhB absorbance around 553 nm using the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples under visible light. The absorption peak obviously diminished with the increase of irradiation time. It was found that only 14% RhB is degraded by the pure TiO2 in 30 min, whereas using the TiO2-peroxo and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts, RhB removal efficiency increased to 54% and 99%, respectively.

Photodegradation rate constants of RhB were calculated by the quasi-first-order kinetics, as the results are illustrated in Fig. 7 a. The rate constant over the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite is 1211 × 10−4 min−1, whereas that of the pristine TiO2 is 15.3 × 10−4 min−1 and that of the TiO2-peroxo is 362 × 10−4 min−1. Therefore, the removal efficiency of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite is almost 79 times of the TiO2 and 3.3 times of the TiO2-peroxo powder, respectively, suggesting that the treatment of TiO2 with H2O2 and integration with C-Dots can considerably enhance the photocatalytic performance under visible light. To display the extent of electron/hole suppression from recombination, PL analysis was performed for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts (Fig. 7b). The excitation wavelength of the PL spectra was 325 nm. As seen, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite displayed the lowest PL intensity than those of the TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo samples, implying that the recombination of charge carriers in the binary nanocomposite has been dramatically decreased, which leaded to boosting photocatalytic performance. Additionally, transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) revealed the effective separation of the charge carriers. As seen, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) sample showed larger photocurrent intensity than the TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo photocatalysts, suggesting the high separation rate of charge carriers. Moreover, the fitted semicircle diameter based on the EIS Nyquist plots displayed that the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots nanocomposite afford smaller arc radius, further confirming the higher transport efficiency of electrons (Fig. 7d). The BET analysis was performed to specify the textural properties of the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples. As can be seen from Fig. 7e, nitrogen isotherm of the pure TiO2 displayed typical type IV isotherm, which represents the mesoporous structure. Whereas the TiO2-peroxo and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts exhibited type I isotherms, indicating the existence of micropores. In addition, the corresponding pore size distribution curves for the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) samples were deduced using the BJH method (Fig. 7f). The surface area of the TiO2, TiO2-peroxo, and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts were 45.8, 174.8, and 267.2 m2/g, respectively. The structural parameters were calculated from the BET analyses are displayed in Table 2 . Indeed, the higher surface area of the TiO2-peroxo and TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalysts can provide more active sites for the photodegradation reaction, leading to premier photocatalytic performance.

The PL spectra of C-Dots solution are displayed in Fig. 8 , which demonstrate superior up-converted photoluminescence feature of this sample. When C-Dots were excited by long-wavelength light (from 500 nm to 900 nm), the up-converted emissions were significantly located in the range from 350 nm to 700 nm. These results imply that C-Dots might enhance the photocatalytic efficiency via the conversion of long wavelengths to short ones, which significantly promotes the light utilization of the photocatalysts.

According to the experimental results, we suggested a plausible mechanism for the photodegradation reaction on the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts. The interaction between H2O2 and TiO2 increases the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. The increased photocatalytic activity of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts can be attributed to narrowing bang gap energy of TiO2, due to treatment with H2O2 molecules by providing peroxo groups over the surface of TiO2. Also, C-Dots can absorb visible and near-infrared lights and convert them to shorter wavelengths. Thus, C-Dots produced UV photons needed for excitation of TiO2 photocatalyst by up-conversion characteristics. Also, C-Dots act as electron sinks to trap a part of photoelectrons from the CB of TiO2-peroxo. In addition, some of the electrons on the CB of TiO2-peroxo and over C-Dots can react with adsorbed O2 to generate
O2 − radicals, as main reactive species. Moreover, h+ in the VB of TiO2-peroxo could react with OH− ions to produce highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (
OH), due to their high oxidation potential (+2.4 eV) than that of
OH/OH− (+1.9 eV), which are highly responsible for the degradation of dyes, as confirmed by scavenging experiments [46].

To clarify the photocatalytic mechanism over the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst, the scavenging experiments were performed. In this study, benzoquinone (BQ,
O2 − scavenger), 2-propanol (2-PrOH,
OH scavenger), and ammonium oxalate (AO, h+ scavenger) were used to reveal which oxidative species are involved in the degradation reaction. The concentration of all scavengers was fixed as 10−3 M. As shown in Fig. 11
, the photodegradation of RhB was obviously reduced after the addition of the selected scavengers. However, after introducing 2-PrOH and BQ, the degradation of RhB was substantially diminished, which revealed that
OH and
O2 − radicals are the main reactive species in the degradation reaction. Further, the results displayed that in the presence of AO, the photocatalytic efficiency of RhB was not substantially decreased, which demonstrated that h+ performed a minor effect on the photocatalytic performance. As a result, the proposed mechanism in Fig. 10 is confirmed by the scavenging experiments.

The durability of photocatalysts is an essential issue for practical utilization. Thus, reusability of the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite for degradation of RhB was investigated for four consecutive cycles, as displayed in Fig. 12 . The results revealed that the binary photocatalyst has virtually no decline of activity after four runs. Hence, this nanocomposite can be supposed as a promising photocatalyst for water purification.

To further investigate the practical applications of the binary photocatalyst, photodegradation of one anionic dye (MO), and two cationic dyes (MB, and Fuchsine), and photoreduction of Cr (VI) were carried out, which are depicted in Fig. 13 . The results revealed that the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite possesses superior photocatalytic performances than the TiO2 and TiO2-peroxo photocatalysts. Photocatalytic activity of this nanocomposite is 79.2, 17.1, 71.4, 40.5, and 21.8 times larger than that of the TiO2 and 3.3, 3, 2.3, 1.81, and 1.77 folds as much as the TiO2-peroxo for degradations of RhB, MO, MB, fuchsine, and photoreduction Cr(VI), respectively. Therefore, it is inferred that the binary TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) photocatalyst has exceptional activity in photocatalytic removal of organic and inorganic pollutants from water under visible light. Furthermore, to display the extent for adsorption of the pollutants over the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite, the dark experiments were performed. It is observable that the pollutants have small adsorption over this nanocomposite. Hence, the decrease of the pollutant during the photocatalytic performances in the presence of the nanocomposite is attributed to the photocatalytic removal reactions rather than the simple adsorption process.

Briefly, in this work, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots photocatalysts were fabricated by a facile procedure. The TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite showed the highest photocatalytic ability for degradations of RhB, MO, MB, and fuchsine and photoreduction Cr(VI), exceeding that of the pure TiO2 by a factor of 79.2, 17.1, 71.4, 40.5, and 21.8, and the TiO2-peroxo by a factor of 3.3, 3, 2.3, 1.81, and 1.77, respectively. The improved photoactivity of the well-designed binary nanocomposite was attributed to the up-conversion characteristics of C-Dots, high visible-light harvesting feature of H2O2 treated TiO2, and improved surface area. In addition, the TiO2-peroxo/C-Dots (0.75 mL) nanocomposite presented high stability and durability, which is important for the potential applications in environmental protection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very thankful for the financial support from University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Iran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2019.03.014.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Uniform carbon dots@TiO2 nanotube arrays with full spectrum wavelength light activation for efficient dye degradation and overall water splitting
